orbit_connections.name: Theodore Roosevelt (X00744) — married great-granddaughter of Daniel Tyler's parents
orbit_connections.relationship: Through his parents Sarah Edwards Tyler (H00936) and Capt. Daniel Tyler, Brig. Gen. Tyler is an ancestor of Edith Kermit Carow (1861-1948), who became Theodore Roosevelt's second wife and First Lady.

notable.notable_blurb: Union Civil War general and ironmaster

cross_connections.display_label: built an iron empire in Gilded Age Los Angeles
was his nephew and rose to Union general too

bio.bio_blurb: Union general, ironmaster, founder of Anniston

military_service.unit: 1st Connecticut Infantry; division, Army of Northeastern Virginia
military_service.notes: Commanded a division at First Bull Run; led a brigade at the Siege of Corinth; briefly commanded Camp Douglas.

narrative_blocks.header: Blamed for the Union rout at Bull Run
narrative_blocks.body: Tyler commanded a division at the First Battle of Bull Run in 1861 and absorbed much of the blame for the Union defeat, mustered out within weeks. Recommissioned in 1862, he led troops at the siege of Corinth and briefly ran the Camp Douglas prison camp.

narrative_blocks.header: He founded the Alabama city of Anniston
narrative_blocks.body: After resigning from the army in 1864, Tyler built blast furnaces and ran railroads, finding far more success in rails than in pig iron. In the 1870s he established the Woodstock Iron Company and helped found Anniston, named for his daughter-in-law Annie.

narrative_blocks.header: West Point artillerist who studied in France
narrative_blocks.body: Graduating from West Point in 1819, Tyler became an authority on artillery, studying at the French school at Metz and translating French gunnery manuals into English. His honesty inspecting private arms contractors may have stalled his promotion beyond first lieutenant.

bio.bio_blurb: Union general, ironmaster, founder of Anniston

military_service.unit: 1st Connecticut Infantry; division, Army of Northeastern Virginia
military_service.notes: Commanded a division at First Bull Run; led a brigade at the Siege of Corinth; briefly commanded Camp Douglas.

narrative_blocks.header: Blamed for the Union rout at Bull Run
narrative_blocks.body: Tyler commanded a division at the First Battle of Bull Run in 1861 and absorbed much of the blame for the Union defeat, mustered out within weeks. Recommissioned in 1862, he led troops at the siege of Corinth and briefly ran the Camp Douglas prison camp.

narrative_blocks.header: He founded the Alabama city of Anniston
narrative_blocks.body: After resigning from the army in 1864, Tyler built blast furnaces and ran railroads, finding far more success in rails than in pig iron. In the 1870s he established the Woodstock Iron Company and helped found Anniston, named for his daughter-in-law Annie.

narrative_blocks.header: West Point artillerist who studied in France
narrative_blocks.body: Graduating from West Point in 1819, Tyler became an authority on artillery, studying at the French school at Metz and translating French gunnery manuals into English. His honesty inspecting private arms contractors may have stalled his promotion beyond first lieutenant.

narrative_blocks.header: Anniston was named for his wife Annie
narrative_blocks.body: Alfred ran the Anniston Manufacturing Company in the Alabama iron town his father helped found. The new city took its name from his wife, Annie Scott Tyler, originally 'Annie's Town.'

narrative_blocks.header: The Alabama city of Anniston bears her name
narrative_blocks.body: Annie Scott married Alfred Lee Tyler in 1859, and the iron town platted by the Tyler family's Woodstock Iron Company was named in her honor. She lived in Anniston until her death in 1914.

narrative_blocks.header: From Paris polish to genteel poverty
narrative_blocks.body: Educated in Paris during her father the general’s European years, Gertrude married the shipping heir Charles Carow in 1859 — and watched his drinking dissolve the family fortune. Widowed in 1883, she eventually moved to Italy, where living was cheaper.

narrative_blocks.header: Her daughter grew up with the Roosevelts
narrative_blocks.body: The Carows were intimates of the East 20th Street Roosevelts, and her daughter Edith was raised in and out of Theodore’s nursery — becoming First Lady in 1901, six years after Gertrude died at Turin.

cross_connections.display_label: was the father of her husband Charles Carow
was the mother of her husband Charles Carow

bio.bio_blurb: Mother of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

notable.notable_blurb: Shipowner; father of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: He owned the packet ship West Point
narrative_blocks.body: Carow inherited the Kermit and Carow shipping line from Robert Kermit, his father's partner, and owned the Liverpool packet West Point through the 1860s. The Civil War's collapse of American shipping ruined the trade and his fortunes declined.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter became a president's wife
narrative_blocks.body: The Carows lived beside the Roosevelts in New York, and his daughter Edith grew up alongside young Theodore. She married him in 1886 and became First Lady fifteen years later.

bio.bio_blurb: Shipowner; father of First Lady Edith Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: Robert died young
narrative_blocks.body: Robert died in 1860, in his first year.

notable.notable_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

narrative_blocks.header: She built the modern First Lady's office
narrative_blocks.body: Edith hired the first salaried social secretary for a First Lady and directed the 1902 White House renovation that created the West Wing. Historians credit her with shaping the modern institution of the role.

narrative_blocks.header: She burned a lifetime of love letters
narrative_blocks.body: After Theodore died, the intensely private Edith destroyed the letters he had written her across their courtship and marriage. It is part of why her inner life stayed, by her own design, almost unknowable.

narrative_blocks.header: She wrote her own Connecticut ancestors' history
narrative_blocks.body: Late in life Edith researched her mother's Tyler family and co-wrote a history of their colonial Connecticut roots. She bought the family's ancestral home in Brooklyn, Connecticut, returning there each summer.

narrative_blocks.header: She stumped for Hoover to disown Franklin
narrative_blocks.body: Edith kept out of politics for decades, then made a rare appearance for Herbert Hoover in 1932. Part of her motive was to remind voters that the Democratic nominee, Franklin Roosevelt, was only a distant cousin and not her son.

narrative_blocks.header: Theodore regretted overruling her judgment
narrative_blocks.body: A childhood neighbor whom Theodore Roosevelt married in 1886, Edith ran Sagamore Hill and raised six children while quietly advising him on people and politics. Roosevelt admitted he was worse off whenever he ignored her counsel.

cross_connections.display_label: was her stepdaughter, the capital’s sharpest wit
her husband President Roosevelt's Hartford-born mother

bio.bio_blurb: First Lady; wife of Theodore Roosevelt

career.role: First Lady of the United States

narrative_blocks.header: The aunt who stayed in Italy
narrative_blocks.body: Unmarried and settled at Porto Maurizio on the Italian Riviera, Emily was the expatriate aunt of the Roosevelt White House children, crossing the Atlantic for family occasions. Her sister the First Lady remained her closest tie.

narrative_blocks.header: A wedding seven months before death
narrative_blocks.body: At sixty-seven, Major Tyler married Belle Alston Webb in Washington on March 16, 1906 — his only known wife — and died that October in Anniston. His 1904 will had already named their two daughters, Lucy Mason and Nellie Osgood Tyler, by their full maiden names.

narrative_blocks.header: He shared his birthday with Augustus
narrative_blocks.body: Edmund was born May 2, 1838; his youngest brother Augustus arrived May 2, 1851 — the same date, thirteen years apart, in the same Norwich household.

bio.bio_blurb: Major who settled in Tyler-founded Anniston

narrative_blocks.header: She married the Irish captain at thirty-five
narrative_blocks.body: The general’s daughter married Captain Alexander Moore in Manhattan in April 1875; their only child, born two years later in Montgomery, Alabama, became founding commandant of the Army’s School of Fire. She lies beside her husband at Arlington.

notable.notable_blurb: Civil War staff officer; Powder River battalion commander

military_service.unit: 13th Wisconsin Infantry; aide-de-camp to Kearny, Hooker, Sickles, Torbert
military_service.notes: Brevets to Major and Lieutenant Colonel for the campaigns before Gettysburg; Colonel for Gettysburg; mustered out Sept 26, 1866

military_service.unit: 38th US Infantry 1867; Company F, 3rd US Cavalry from 1870; commanded 5th Battalion, Big Horn Expedition
military_service.notes: Court-martialed after Powder River, March 17, 1876; sentence remitted by the President; resigned Aug 10, 1879

narrative_blocks.header: General Hooker gave him orders
narrative_blocks.body: At Antietam on September 17, 1862, he rode the field as Hooker’s aide-de-camp, carrying the First Corps’ attack orders under fire until the general was carried off wounded — service the brevets later honored as “gallant and meritorious.” From Fredericksburg through Chancellorsville he bore the orders of a corps, a grand division, and finally the Army of the Potomac itself.

narrative_blocks.header: He married into the real Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: The Hooker whose orders he carried was “Fighting Joe” — a different Hooker line entirely, no relation to the Rev. Thomas Hooker family of this tree. Moore’s true tie came in 1875, when he married Gen. Daniel Tyler’s daughter Mary Law, a Hooker descendant.

narrative_blocks.header: In the Peach Orchard when Sickles fell
narrative_blocks.body: On Daniel Sickles’s staff during the Gettysburg campaign, Moore was present in the Peach Orchard on July 2, 1863, when a Confederate shell took the general’s right leg. Brevets for gallantry followed — major, lieutenant colonel, and colonel.

narrative_blocks.header: Court-martialed at Powder River, then remitted
narrative_blocks.body: Commanding the 5th Battalion at the Battle of Powder River in March 1876 — the strike on Crazy Horse’s village — he was court-martialed for failing to support the attack; the President remitted the sentence, and Moore resigned in 1879 to ranch in Guadalupe County, Texas.

narrative_blocks.header: Nicknamed for a fictional Irish dragoon
narrative_blocks.body: Hooker’s staff called the Ballymoney-born aide “the Charley O’Malley of the army” — after the dashing Irish cavalry hero of Charles Lever’s bestselling 1841 novel. Hooker thought the name apt “except in one respect: I doubt if your prototype ever possessed the confidence of his commander to the degree you had mine.”

cross_connections.display_label: was his brother-in-law and fellow Powder River campaigner

bio.bio_blurb: Founder of the Army’s artillery school

career.organization: US Army School of Fire, Fort Sill

narrative_blocks.header: He founded the Army’s artillery school
narrative_blocks.body: In 1911 Moore became founder and first commandant of the US Army School of Fire at Fort Sill, Oklahoma — today’s Field Artillery School. He had served as White House military aide to Theodore Roosevelt, husband of his first cousin Edith.

narrative_blocks.header: Married in Lucerne between White House seasons
narrative_blocks.body: Washington society followed the aide’s November 1906 wedding to Luvie Butler in Lucerne, Switzerland — two months’ leave, returning “just at the opening of the social season.” She died in 1915, leaving him three children under ten.

narrative_blocks.header: First in French, far from it in drawing
narrative_blocks.body: At West Point he stood first in French and Spanish “without having to work very hard” and was far from distinguished in drawing, his classmate Dorst remembered. Five frontier years with the 4th Cavalry followed — scouting from Fort Clark’s armed camps, where no one walked two hundred yards unarmed.

narrative_blocks.header: Snow-blind on Mackenzie’s winter expedition
narrative_blocks.body: A volunteer on Crook’s Powder River Expedition after the Custer disaster, he rode in Mackenzie’s November 1876 destruction of the Cheyenne village — and left on sick leave, his eyes burned by glare off the snow. He resigned in 1878, “regretted, loved and respected.”

narrative_blocks.header: Seven thousand acres of American tea
narrative_blocks.body: Commanding the 3rd Connecticut Volunteers in South Carolina in 1898, the colonel saw commercial promise in southern tea and bought four Colleton County plantations — Holly Grove, Rutledge Island, Mowbray, Turkey Hill — whose flourishing gardens and factory his son Frederick managed.

narrative_blocks.header: The Russian ambassador took his house
narrative_blocks.body: The stately residence he built at 1634 I Street, facing Farragut Square in Washington, later housed the Russian ambassador. He was president of New London’s Pequot Casino Association at his death, a clubman to the last afternoon.

narrative_blocks.header: Born on his brother’s thirteenth birthday
narrative_blocks.body: May 2, 1851 — thirteen years to the day after his brother Edmund, born May 2, 1838. The Tyler brothers shared a birthday a generation apart.

cross_connections.display_label: was the patent-medicine magnate father of his wife Cornelia
fought at Powder River in March; he rode there in November

bio.bio_blurb: Cavalry officer turned American tea planter

military_service.notes: Texas frontier 1873-78; volunteer on Crook’s Powder River Expedition, Nov-Dec 1876; resigned July 1, 1878
Camps Haven, Meade, and Marion; honorably discharged Jan 31, 1899

career.organization: American Tea Growing Co., Colleton County SC

bio.bio_blurb: First woman to write Broadway comic opera

narrative_blocks.header: The first woman to write Broadway comic opera
narrative_blocks.body: Her A Venetian Romance at the Knickerbocker Theater made her the first woman to author a comic opera for a Broadway hearing — though she was “too modest to allow her photographs to be used for the public prints.” Richard Mansfield, a frequent houseguest, pushed the piece to production.

narrative_blocks.header: The dancing-master’s son at her piano
narrative_blocks.body: The boy pianist hired for her daughters’ dance party turned out to be the son of the man who had taught her dancing as a girl in Norwich. She installed Frederic Coit Wright as household musician, paid for his training, and wrote the opera with him.

narrative_blocks.header: Lady Bountiful of the Pequot colony
narrative_blocks.body: Her summer home, The Elm, anchored New London’s aristocratic Pequot colony with the most splendid entertainments; the town called her Lady Bountiful for her charity. Yet she refused to let a single photograph reach the public prints.

bio.bio_blurb: Namesake of a League of Women Voters award

narrative_blocks.header: An award still carries her name
narrative_blocks.body: The New London League of Women Voters established the Edna L. Tyler Award at Mitchell College, honoring a student’s “informed and active participation” in self-government. She had grown up facing Farragut Square, in the I Street house her father built.

career.organization: family tea plantations, Colleton County SC

narrative_blocks.header: From tea gardens to the cotton mills
narrative_blocks.body: He managed his father’s flourishing Colleton County tea gardens and factory, then led Anniston Manufacturing as president — a leading industrialist of the South across thirty-three Anniston years.

H00381.prominent_name_reason: Hon. Timothy Edwards (1738-1813) — Princeton 1757; merchant and judge of Stockbridge MA. His son "Edward Hooker Edwards" (b.1763) carried the Hooker name explicitly — a deliberate tribute to the founding ancestor in the 5th generation of Edwards descendants.

narrative_blocks.header: Princeton 1757 — son of Jonathan Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Princeton in 1757 and went to Elizabethtown, New Jersey to practice law. He moved to Stockbridge, Massachusetts around 1770, where he served as a judge — earning the 'Hon.' style on his name.

narrative_blocks.header: Married into the Ogden family of New Jersey
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Rhoda Ogden was sister of Aaron Ogden (Governor of New Jersey 1812-1813) and Matthias Ogden, a lieutenant colonel who marched to Quebec with Benedict Arnold in 1775. Through her, the Edwards-Hooker line connected to early New Jersey political and military leadership.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter Phebe married a Hooker — double convergence
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Phebe Edwards married Rev. Asahel Hooker — both descended from Thomas Hooker, creating a double Hooker convergence in their children. Phebe's path back went through her mother Sarah Pierpont Edwards; Asahel's through the main Hartford Hooker line.

INST035.name: United States Military Academy (West Point)
INST035.notes: Founded 1802 at West Point NY. HOOKER TREE CONNECTION: Rev. George Washington Woodbridge (HD2163), son of General Jonathan Woodbridge (H00928), graduated West Point; served as 2nd Lieutenant US Army 1826-1829 before entering the Episcopal ministry. He became rector of Monumental Church Richmond VA 1843-1878 and escorted Flora Cooke Stuart to her dying husband Jeb Stuart in May 1864.
